content: Hey Saturday, we're in the final stretch with Project Kestrel and need to lock down our infrastructure before September 8th. I've been reviewing the production database setup and want to confirm we're solid on our RDS configuration. We're planning 1 primary with 2 read replicas in Multi-AZ, which should handle our expected load. Can you walk me through the failover strategy and whether we need any additional monitoring on the database layer?

Great question — your RDS setup is solid for launch. With Multi-AZ enabled, if your primary instance fails, AWS automatically promotes one of the read replicas within 2-3 minutes, and DNS is updated automatically. For your 2 read replicas, I'd recommend distributing read traffic via a reader endpoint to balance queries. On the monitoring front, you'll want to watch RDS CPU, connection count, and replication lag in Datadog. Since you're running 15-day log retention, make sure to set critical database alerts (CPU > 80%, replication lag > 5 seconds) to trigger pages before you hit issues. Have you configured parameter groups for both instances yet, and are you using IAM authentication for connections?

Good point about the reader endpoint — we haven't finalized parameter groups yet, but that's on our list for this week. Actually, one thing I'm less clear on: we went with Cloudflare for our CDN over Fastly and CloudFront. The price argument won out, but I want to make sure we're not sacrificing reliability or performance. What should we be testing before go-live, especially around cache invalidation and regional failover?

Cloudflare was the right call financially, and honestly their reliability is rock-solid — they have edge servers in 200+ cities worldwide. For pre-launch testing, focus on three areas: First, validate cache invalidation workflows — test purging single files, directories, and cache-tags to ensure content updates propagate within 30 seconds across regions. Second, set up health checks on your origin servers through Cloudflare's dashboard so it auto-fails over if your primary goes down. Third, load test through Cloudflare's network using a tool like Apache JMeter from multiple geographic regions to spot any latency or TLS handshake issues. One thing to verify: do you have your origin server's IP whitelisted and the Host header properly configured? Cloudflare can be finicky about those details.

We've got the origin IP whitelisted and Host header configured, good call. Now let me ask about our monitoring — we've settled on Datadog APM with 15-day log retention, but I'm wondering if that's going to be enough depth for a launch week. We're probably going to see higher-than-normal traffic spikes and unusual usage patterns from new users. Should we consider extending retention temporarily, or do you think 15 days covers us?

Fifteen days is a reasonable baseline, but here's my honest take: during launch week you'll generate 3-4x normal log volume, and you'll absolutely want to dig into edge cases and user behavior patterns. I'd suggest temporarily bumping retention to 30 days for your first month post-launch — the cost difference is usually minimal compared to the peace of mind. More importantly, set up Datadog dashboards now for key metrics: request latency (p50, p95, p99), error rates by endpoint, database query performance, and Cloudflare cache hit ratio. Create a separate alert group for launch week so critical issues page immediately. Also, enable distributed tracing so you can trace a single request through your entire stack — that's been a lifesaver in past launches I've worked on.

Okay, that makes sense. Let's talk about launch timing — September 8th is confirmed for Monday morning at 09:00 CEST, but I'm realizing we haven't really nailed down our runbook and go-live checklist. What should we be documenting before we flip the switch? And honestly, should we even be doing a full hard launch or do a soft launch first with internal users?

I'd absolutely recommend a soft launch the week before — go-live September 1st, Monday, with internal users and a small group of beta testers only. This gives you a full week to catch edge cases, performance issues, and unexpected behavior without the pressure of public users hammering you. For your runbook, document: database backup and restore procedures, Cloudflare cache purge workflows, how to scale RDS read replicas on demand, rollback procedures for code deployments, and incident escalation paths with names and phone numbers. Your go-live checklist should cover: verify database replication is synced, confirm Datadog alerts are active, do a test failover, load test one more time at 80% expected peak capacity, and have a war room setup with Slack channel and video call. Create a 'launch day roles' document too — who owns database, who owns CDN, who monitors Datadog, etc.

The soft launch timing makes a lot of sense — gives us buffer room for surprises. Quick question on the runbook: should we document different scenarios like 'database failover mid-launch' or 'Cloudflare goes down' separately, or just create one master runbook with all failure modes? Also, we haven't talked about customer communication yet. When do we start telling people about the September 8th launch date?

Definitely create scenario-specific runbooks instead of one massive document — people panic under pressure and can't parse a 50-page Bible. Create focused docs: 'RDS Failover Playbook' (2-3 pages), 'Cloudflare Incident Response' (1-2 pages), 'Rollback Procedure' (1 page), and 'How to Scale Read Replicas' (1 page). Each should have clear decision trees and pre-written commands to execute. As for customer communication, I'd suggest a staggered approach: announce the launch date to existing customers (those in beta or on waitlists) about 3-4 weeks before — September 1st, so maybe mid-August communication. Give them a sneak peek during the soft launch week if they're part of your beta group. For the general public, do a bigger announcement on September 6th, Friday — two days before public launch to build hype but not so far out that they forget. What's your current beta user count?

We've got about 40-50 beta users spread across different regions, which is perfect for the soft launch validation. One thing that just occurred to me — with the soft launch on September 1st and hard launch on September 8th, we're running parallel infrastructure for a week. Do we need separate database environments, or can we use the same RDS instances for both?

Good thinking — you can absolutely run both on the same RDS instances. Just make sure you have separate schemas or database names: one for 'kestrel_staging' and one for 'kestrel_production'. That way soft launch traffic (your 40-50 beta users) hits staging, and you keep production completely isolated for the real launch on September 8th. This is actually better because you'll catch any schema migration issues, performance bottlenecks, or data integrity problems in staging before they hit production. Configure your application to read a feature flag or environment variable to route traffic correctly — beta users should never touch production databases. Also, set up Datadog monitors on both environments separately so you can compare behavior side-by-side. Staging metrics will give you a blueprint for what production should look like when the floodgates open on the 8th.

That staging/production split makes total sense. Now I'm thinking about our Cloudflare configuration — should we be using different Cloudflare zones for staging versus production, or can we manage it with subdomain routing? I'm also wondering if we need to do SSL certificate validation before launch, especially since we'll be handling potentially sensitive data.

For Cloudflare, using separate zones is overkill and costs extra — just use subdomains like staging.kestrel.app and app.kestrel.app, both pointing to the same Cloudflare account but with different DNS records pointing to different origins. This keeps your setup clean and lets you tweak cache rules per subdomain easily. On SSL certs, this is critical: get a wildcard or multi-domain certificate (*.kestrel.app) issued well before launch — I'd say by August 25th to give buffer time for any validation hiccups. Use Let's Encrypt for free or your preferred CA, and make sure your renewal automation is set up so you never face an expired cert situation. On launch day, do a test: hit your staging subdomain and production subdomain from multiple browsers/regions and verify the SSL handshake works and there are no cert warnings. Cloudflare should handle TLS termination, but double-check your origin server is also correctly configured for your domain.

Got it — we'll lock down the SSL cert by late August. Here's one more thing that came up in our architecture review: our read replica setup — are we automatically routing read queries to the reader endpoint in our application code, or do we need a middleware/proxy layer to manage that? I want to make sure we're not accidentally hammering the primary with read requests.

You want to configure it in your application code using RDS reader endpoints rather than adding middleware — it's simpler and faster. Most ORMs (Sequelize, SQLAlchemy, Prisma, etc.) support replica configuration natively. Set up two connection pools: one pointing to your primary instance for writes, one pointing to the RDS reader endpoint for reads. Your application should route SELECT queries to the reader endpoint and INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE to primary. This is usually done with a replication-aware ORM setting or a custom query wrapper. One critical caveat: be careful with read-after-write consistency — if a user creates a record and immediately queries it, they might hit a replica that hasn't yet replicated the data. For critical user flows, route those reads back to the primary temporarily or implement application-level caching. Have you identified which queries are read-heavy and which tables need to go to the reader endpoint?

We're mostly using Prisma as our ORM, so I know it has built-in replica support. We haven't fully mapped out which queries hit replicas yet, but that's definitely on the to-do before September 8th. Let me zoom out a bit — I feel like we're nailing infrastructure, but have we properly thought through what happens if something catastrophic breaks during the soft launch week? Like, what's our decision tree for deciding to postpone the hard launch?

Excellent question — this is where a launch decision matrix becomes invaluable. Create a document now that defines hard stops vs. acceptable risk. Hard stops (postpone launch): database replication lag exceeds 30 seconds consistently, Cloudflare or primary origin down for >5 minutes during testing, any unresolved critical security vulnerability discovered, or data corruption in production schema. Acceptable with mitigation: minor performance issues (up to p99 latency <2s) that you can fix within 24 hours, single non-critical endpoint errors you can patch after launch, or cosmetic bugs. During the soft launch week, run daily standups (15 minutes) reviewing Datadog metrics, user feedback, and error logs. By September 6th, you should make a go/no-go decision. If you hit a hard stop, postponing to September 15th is fine — better than a botched launch. Have you assigned a launch day commander who has final say on go/no-go, and does your leadership team understand that postponement is an option if needed?

We haven't formally assigned a launch commander yet, but that's something we need to do this week. I'm thinking it should be someone technical who understands the full stack but also has buy-in from leadership. One last thing — our current Datadog setup with 15-day retention and APM — do we need to configure any special alerts or dashboards specific to launch day itself, beyond what we already discussed?

Absolutely — create a dedicated 'Launch Day' dashboard in Datadog that shows: overall request throughput, error rate %, database connection pool usage, Cloudflare cache hit ratio, and API response latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99). Set up a launch-specific alert group that pages immediately on: error rate > 5%, p99 latency > 3 seconds, database connections > 80% of pool, RDS CPU > 85%, or any critical exceptions. Have a separate Slack channel (#kestrel-launch) where Datadog posts all alerts in real-time. For the launch day commander role, pick someone with infrastructure background (could be you?) who can make quick decisions without needing committee approval. They should have a pre-written 'rollback decision script' — if error rate stays above 5% for 15 minutes after launch, they have authority to trigger rollback. Finally, schedule your launch window for 09:00 CEST on Monday the 8th, but have your team in the war room starting 08:00 to do final checks. Give yourself a 2-hour window to validate the system before you consider the launch 'successful.' Sound good?
